feedback.text: Esteemed Members of the European Commission, I believe that the Digital Fairness Act is a step in the right direction for citizens across the European Union. The EU has long stood as a defender of consumer rights, and it is in that spirit that I wish to bring the following issues to your attention, in the hope that this legislation will become a true shield against abuse and predatory practices. Firstly, I would like to highlight the concerns raised by the Stop Killing Games initiative. Many companies currently sell what they call "licenses" to video games, which as they themselves state can be revoked at any time, for any reason. When I buy a movie or an album, the creator does not come to my house to destroy the DVD or CD whenever they choose. Video games should be treated no differently. It is deeply unfair to charge consumers for a product and then remove access entirely, without any alternative. In some cases, consumers have paid for brand-new games, only for companies to shut them down shortly afterward due to disappointing profits. Secondly, I would like to address an even more concerning practice recently adopted by Visa and Mastercard. These financial institutions have pressured multiple video game platforms into banning games that are perfectly legal, under the threat of cutting off payment processing. We are citizens of sovereign EU member states, governed by our respective national laws and united under the authority of the European Union. It is unacceptable for private corporations with no legal mandate to effectively override democratic law by enforcing their own arbitrary content restrictions. I urge you, as a deeply concerned citizen, to protect the integrity of our legal framework and address this issue in the Digital Fairness Act. Thank you for your attention and for your continued efforts in protecting consumer rights in the digital age.
